does: This paper develops a foundational framework for understanding control in AI systems by anchoring control to the core concept of setting and achieving goals. The authors establish a working definition of control by drawing on established concepts from cybernetics, management control, and control theory, identifying key requirements for maintaining control such as the ability to set goals, maintaining a functional control loop, possessing requisite variety (the ability to respond to system complexity), and ensuring sufficient goal alignment. The paper then systematically analyzes how control can be lost, examining the specific mechanisms through which AI systems can contribute to loss of control across various contexts and scales.
connects: This work builds on foundational control theory and cybernetics literature while addressing a critical gap in AI safety discourse. It extends concepts from management and organizational control systems to the AI domain, drawing on established frameworks like Ashby's law of requisite variety. The paper responds to and critiques existing AI loss of control discussions in academic, industry, and policy contexts that lack rigorous definitional grounding. It connects to broader AI safety research on alignment and robustness, while providing the conceptual scaffolding that much of this work implicitly assumes but rarely makes explicit. The framework also relates to risk analysis literature by providing clearer ontological foundations for risk categorization and assessment.
enables: This foundational framework enables more rigorous and systematic analysis of diverse loss of control scenarios ranging from individual systems to large-scale societal risks. It provides researchers, policymakers, and safety practitioners with precise conceptual tools to diagnose where control failures might occur and what interventions could restore or maintain control. The work supports development of better loss of control risk assessments and mitigation strategies by clarifying which control requirements are most critical in different contexts. It potentially enables cross-disciplinary dialogue by translating between control-theoretic language and AI safety discourse, facilitating integration of classical control solutions into modern AI governance and technical safety work.
